questions.text: When learning a new concept in class, you prefer:
opts: Diagrams, charts, or visual presentations on the board
Teacher explaining it verbally in detail
Reading from textbooks or written notes
Doing hands-on activities or experiments

questions.text: If you need to assemble something (like a project model), you would:
opts: Look at the picture or diagram provided
Ask someone to explain it step-by-step
Read the instructions carefully first
Start building and figure it out as you go

questions.text: When you get lost in a new place, you typically:
opts: Look at a map or GPS
Ask someone for directions verbally
Read street signs and written directions
Wander around and explore until you find it

questions.text: When remembering important information (like exam dates), you usually:
opts: Visualize it in your mind or write it in color-coded notes
Repeat it aloud several times
Write it down in a planner or notebook
Practice the information repeatedly

questions.text: During a subject like Physics or Chemistry, you learn best through:
opts: Diagrams, molecular models, or flowcharts
Teacher's explanations and class discussions
Reading chapters and working through examples
Lab experiments and practical demonstrations

questions.text: When working on a group project, your role is usually to:
opts: Create visuals, posters, or PowerPoint slides
Lead discussions and present findings to others
Write the content and organize the presentation
Build models or create physical demonstrations

questions.text: For a History or Social Studies topic, you prefer to learn by:
opts: Watching documentaries or viewing maps/timelines
Listening to stories and discussions about events
Reading historical accounts and taking detailed notes
Visiting museums or re-enacting historical events

questions.text: When you struggle with a concept, you tend to:
opts: Draw pictures or create mind maps to understand it
Ask your teacher or friends to explain it verbally
Look for additional reading materials or examples
Practice with real examples or work through problems

questions.text: Your ideal study environment includes:
opts: Good lighting, organized desk, color-coded materials
Quiet place, or studying with a study buddy to discuss
Books, notes, and written resources all around
Space to move around, fidget tools, or take breaks

questions.text: When learning a new language (English, Hindi, etc.), you do best with:
opts: Written examples and reading comprehension
Listening to native speakers and speaking practice
Grammar rules and written exercises
Role-playing conversations and practical usage

questions.text: During online classes, you find it easier to focus when:
opts: There are slides with visuals and clear structure
The teacher speaks clearly and there's interactive discussion
Detailed notes or transcripts are provided
There are activities, quizzes, or things to do

questions.text: After a lesson, you best retain information by:
opts: Creating summaries with diagrams and colors
Discussing it with classmates or explaining to others
Re-reading and rewriting notes
Applying it through problems, projects, or practice

questions.text: I am able to adapt to change
When faced with a difficult exam or assignment, I have a plan to tackle it
I tend to bounce back after a failure or disappointment
I believe I can find solutions to my problems, even during tough times
Staying focused on my goals helps me get through stressful periods
I have the ability to control how I respond to difficult situations
I look for creative solutions when facing obstacles
Facing pressure or competition brings out my best efforts
I have people I can rely on during difficult times
I learn from my mistakes rather than dwelling on them
I remain optimistic even when facing major challenges
I take action when there's something I can do about a problem
I find ways to relieve stress and manage my emotions
I can handle criticism without losing confidence
I persist in my efforts even when progress is slow
I have a sense of purpose that guides my decisions
When things don't go as planned, I adapt my approach
I believe that adversity has made me stronger
I often come up with new and unusual ideas
When solving a problem, I explore multiple different approaches
I'm willing to take risks and try unconventional methods
I can combine different ideas to create something completely new
I enjoy working on projects that have no standard solution
I notice connections others might miss between different subjects
I can develop many different solutions for the same problem
When given an everyday object, I can imagine many different uses for it
I express my ideas creatively (art, writing, music, design, etc.)
I enjoy imagining 'what if' scenarios and exploring possibilities
I'm able to improve or refine ideas (my own or others')
I challenge the status quo and question 'why we do things this way'
I can adapt my thinking style based on what a project needs
I enjoy experimenting with new techniques or materials
I can turn a failure or mistake into a learning opportunity or innovation
I am known for bringing fresh perspectives to team projects or discussions
I can inspire others to work toward a common goal
I take responsibility for team outcomes, even if something goes wrong
I can make difficult decisions when others are uncertain
I naturally take initiative to solve problems without being asked
I listen carefully to others' perspectives before making decisions
I can convince others to support my ideas or approach
I help develop the skills and confidence of people around me
I can handle conflict constructively and find win-win solutions
I think strategically about long-term goals and impact
I am comfortable delegating tasks and trust others to execute
I adapt my communication style based on the person or situation
I stay calm and focused under pressure or crisis
I seek feedback and actively work on self-improvement
I follow through on commitments and meet my responsibilities
I can build strong relationships and foster team cohesion
I stand up for what I believe in, even if it's unpopular
